name: Bonus Eventus
description: Bonus Eventus was a Roman agricultural deity, personifying success and good fortune in farming and harvesting. He was specifically associated with the completion of agricultural tasks and the achievement of a bountiful yield. Unlike deities focused on growth or fertility, Bonus Eventus represented the successful culmination of the agricultural process, the reaping of rewards for hard work and dedication. He was often depicted as a young man holding a sheaf of wheat or grapes, symbolizing the abundance he bestowed. While not as prominent as Ceres or Bacchus, Bonus Eventus held a significant place in the Roman agricultural pantheon, reminding farmers to strive for excellence and to appreciate the fruits of their labor. His worship ensured a successful harvest and prevented spoilage, ensuring the community's prosperity. The deity was seen as a guarantor of positive outcomes in endeavors requiring diligent effort and proper timing. He was invoked during harvest festivals and celebrated for his role in securing the food supply of Rome. His existence highlights the Roman emphasis on practical success and the importance of agriculture in their society.
subtitle: God of Successful Harvests

keyMyths.title: Invocation for Abundance
keyMyths.description: Farmers would invoke Bonus Eventus during the harvest season, praying for his blessing to ensure a plentiful yield and prevent spoilage. This ritual was crucial for the community's food security.

keyMyths.title: Statues in Agricultural Temples
keyMyths.description: Statues of Bonus Eventus were often placed in temples dedicated to agricultural deities, emphasizing his role in completing the cycle of growth and ensuring a successful outcome. These statues served as visual reminders of his importance.

keyMyths.title: Offerings of First Fruits
keyMyths.description: The first fruits of the harvest were often offered to Bonus Eventus as a gesture of gratitude and to secure his continued favor. This practice acknowledged his role in the success of the agricultural year.

sources.author: Varro
